content.claim: The specialist workforce shortage in Hungary is regionally concentrated (45 advertised gyógypedagógus positions in Budapest; 2–3 positions each in rural Transdanubian counties), yet enrollment in special-education degree programs rose 15% (to 3,768 first-choice state-scholarship applicants in 2026). However, many part-time correspondence-program students are parents of affected children unlikely to become practicing teachers, meaning rising enrollment does not directly map to future practicing workforce supply.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Geographic workforce distribution data refer to advertised positions, not actual deployment or capacity utilization
inference about part-time student conversion to practice is plausible but not quantified with cohort-tracking data
current staffing levels (4,900 special-education/conductor teachers in primary schools; 949 developmental teachers; 1,404 in traveling networks) are reported as a snapshot without trend information
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
